title: practical english usage - Part 146
content:
►     11) is similar to the order before nouns. Note that noun modifiers are not The  order of adjectives in predicative position (after be, seem and similar verbs, 7 after    a  verb For more details,  ►    296.9.

these new ideas (     thesey-new-ideas) not ten green bottles (     tenrgreen-botties) Commas are not used after numbers or other determiners.

an attractive traditional woollen dress information.

We do not use commas between adjectives which  give different kinds of a tall(,) dark(,) handsome cowboy But commas can be dropped before short common adjectives.

an expensive, ill-planned, wasteful project a steep, slippery, grassy slope sequences) which  give similar kinds of information, as in physical descriptions.

Before nouns, we  generally use commas between adjectives (especially in longer 6 commas a big new car factory  enormous black iron gates follow adjectives.

Note that noun modifiers (which often classify, or refer to material) usually 5  noun    modifiers       after    adjectives my last two jobs the first three days (More common than the three first days) First, next and last most often go before one, two, three, etc.

six large eggs the second big shock Numbers usually go before adjectives.

grammar •  186   adjectives after nouns and pronouns the woman upstairs the people outside Some adverbs can also be used like this.

It's the only solution possible / possible solution.

Send all the tickets available / available tickets. (= ... tickets which This is common with adjectives ending in -ɑble/-ible.

Some adjectives can be used after nouns in a similar way to relative clauses.

2 available,          possible,         etc Poet Laureate court martial (= military court)       Attorney General Secretary General         President elect Adjectives come after nouns in some fixed phrases.

1  fixed    phrases:     Secretary          General;      court      martial Adjectives come immediately after nouns in a few special cases.

186  adjectives       af ter   no  uns   and   pronouns For more information about and, ►    226.

Now just put your gun  down nice and slow.

The work was nice and easy.

It's nice and warm in front of the fire. (= pleasantly warm) adjective or an adverb. It means something like ‘ pleasantly’  or ‘ suitably’ .

In an informal style, the expression nice and is often used before another 4 nice     and    ...

a ridiculous economic policy (     a ridiculous and economic-poliey) not an angry young man (     an^igfy-amlytHmg man) We   do not use and between adjectives that give different kinds of information.

3   and     not   used She's a musical and artistic genius.

It's a social and political problem.

different classes.

We   also use and when we  say that something belongs to two or more hot and cold drinks (= hot drinks and cold drinks) a yellow and black sports car a concrete and glass factory different parts of something, or distinguish different types of thing.

And is necessary when two or more adjectives (or other modifiers) refer to an ill-planned, expensive (and) wasteful project a cruel (and) vicious tyrant a warm (and) generous personality especially when we  are ’ piling up' favourable or unfavourable descriptions.

However, and is possible when the adjectives give similar kinds of information, a big beautiful garden In attributive position (before a noun), and is less common.

2 before     a  noun Section15 Adjectives grammar •  187   adjectives: position after as, how, so, too For the structure with adjective + as in expressions like tired as / was..., ►    255.

Those girls are too kind to refuse, (     They are-loo kindgiflslottfuse;) not I like your country - it's so beautiful, (    LJlke-yourso-beautiful country:) The  structure is not possible without a/an.

It was so warm a day that I could hardly work.

How good a pianist is he?    I couldn 't afford that big a car.

I have as good a voice as you.  She is too polite a person to refuse.

as/how/so/too/this/that + adjective + a/an + noun This structure is common in a formal style.
